coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria completeness and testability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and testability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section the E2E test file path(s) (e.g., `tests/audit/test_ticket_page.py`) and the test framework/runner used, and confirm those files are discovered by `make test`.
evidence: Scope lists only `src/rebar/audit/server.py` and `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`.
Nine of ten ACs specify 'an E2E test' as the Oracle but no test file path or framework is named anywhere in the plan.
AC10 proves coverage via `make test` but the plan does not establish that the E2E tests will be discovered by that command.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists only production source files (`server.py` and templates) but omits the E2E test files that are the primary proving commands for every acceptance criterion. An executor must infer the test file path(s) and the test framework (pytest + test-client? playwright? custom harness) with no guidance, risking tests that don't integrate with `make test`.
findings.location: Scope section / AC10 (`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass)
scenarios: An executor writes E2E tests in a file outside the test discovery path and `make test` passes vacuously.
An executor chooses a framework (e.g., playwright) that requires a separate `make e2e` target not covered by AC10, leaving the ACs unproven by the stated Oracle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one scope bullet: `tests/audit/test_ticket_page.py` — pytest-based E2E tests using the app's existing test-client fixture; discovered by `make test` via the standard pytest discovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify 'scores shown to one decimal' in the Visual system: either name the specific values rendered as text (e.g., `priority` and `block_threshold` shown as decimal labels adjacent to the meter) or remove the rule if no numeric text is rendered.
evidence: 'scores shown to one decimal' — Visual system bullet.
Threshold meter spec: 'a 0–1 bar filled to `priority` with a tick mark at the finding's OWN resolved `block_threshold`' — no mention of rendered numeric text.
No other numeric value is identified in the page spec that would require a decimal-precision rule.
findings.finding: The Visual System bullet 'scores shown to one decimal' is ambiguous: the plan does not state which numeric values are rendered as text on the page nor where they appear. The threshold meter is described as a purely visual bar+tick — it is unclear whether `priority` and/or `block_threshold` are also displayed as decimal numerals alongside the bar, or whether the 'one decimal' rule refers to some other element.
findings.location: What section → Visual system bullet; threshold meter description
scenarios: Executor renders `priority` as a visible '0.7' label next to the bar; another executor omits all numeric text entirely — both can claim compliance.
AC4 asserts tick positions and color classes but says nothing about rendered decimal text, leaving the visual-system rule untestable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'scores shown to one decimal' with: 'The threshold meter renders `priority` and `block_threshold` as small decimal labels (e.g., `0.7 / 0.6`) adjacent to the bar; all other numeric values on the page (counts, indices) are integers.' Or, if no numeric text is intended, remove the sentence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `verdict` (or confirm its absence and specify the derivation rule) to the Dependencies field inventory for `trail['code_reviews']` sidecars, so the gate-strip CODE chip has an unambiguous source.
evidence: Dependencies: 'each sidecar is a code-review payload (the reader accepts BOTH `code_review_result_v1` and `code_review_result_v2`) with SEPARATE buckets `blocking/advisory` (+ `dropped/indeterminate/coaching` on v2)' — `verdict` is absent from this list.
What/Gate strip: 'CODE from the latest sidecar of the code-review artifact in `code_reviews` (its `verdict` + blocking count)'
findings.finding: The gate-status strip's CODE chip is specified to show `verdict` from the code-review sidecar, but the Dependencies section's field inventory for `trail['code_reviews']` never lists a `verdict` field — it lists only `blocking/advisory` (+ `dropped/indeterminate/coaching` on v2) buckets. An implementer reading the Dependencies contract has no basis to expect a top-level `verdict` field on a code-review sidecar payload.
findings.location: Dependencies section ('trail["code_reviews"]' field list) vs. What section ('Gate-status strip (sticky)' — 'CODE from the latest sidecar... its `verdict` + blocking count')
scenarios: Implementer reads the Dependencies contract, finds no `verdict` field on code-review payloads, and must guess whether to synthesise it from bucket emptiness or whether a `verdict` key genuinely exists on the payload — two different implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dependencies section, extend the code-review sidecar description to explicitly list `verdict` as a top-level field (e.g. 'each sidecar carries a top-level `verdict` field plus SEPARATE buckets `blocking/advisory`…') or, if no such field exists, specify the derivation: 'the CODE strip verdict is FAIL when `blocking` is non-empty, PASS when `blocking` is empty and at least one bucket is non-empty, else not-run'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend an existing AC) covering: (a) the description/AC block is hidden behind a collapsible `<details>` toggle in the ticket header; (b) each round header renders a trust caption containing `model` and `impact_model_version` and no timestamp or certified badge.
evidence: What/Ticket header: 'the `description` (which contains the full acceptance-criteria block) behind a "details" toggle (not a persistent side rail). No certification badge is rendered'
What/Trust signals: 'a quiet caption: the gate/model version (`model` + `impact_model_version`) and the round `verdict`. (NO per-round timestamp and NO certified badge)'
ABSENCE: no AC asserts that the description toggle exists, is collapsible, or that model/impact_model_version appear in any round header.
findings.finding: Two described deliverables — the ticket header (collapsible description/AC toggle) and the round-header trust signals (model + impact_model_version caption) — have no covering acceptance criterion. An auditor cannot verify these deliverables are implemented correctly from the AC set alone.
findings.location: What section ('Ticket header (compact, collapsible)' and 'Trust signals') vs. Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers either)
scenarios: A reviewer approves all ACs green but the description toggle is missing or the model-version caption is absent — both pass the AC gate silently.
The trust-signal caption inadvertently shows a timestamp or certified badge (the constraint exists only in the body, not in any testable criterion).
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 or add a new AC: 'The ticket header renders title, status, assignee, and a `<details>` toggle for the description. Each plan/code-review round header renders a trust caption containing the `model` and `impact_model_version` values and no per-round timestamp or certification badge. Oracle: an E2E test asserting the description is inside a `<details>` element and that a round header contains the model string but no timestamp element.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a WCAG 1.4.3 done-definition to the visual system spec: name a minimum contrast ratio (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large/bold text and UI components) and add either a linting rule or an AC asserting the inline CSS palette meets it.
evidence: What/Visual system: 'saturated color reserved for verdict + decision-series chips only (everything else neutral grey); severity is always encoded by glyph + label + color (never color-only); scores shown to one decimal' — no contrast ratio mentioned.
Approach: 'single-column stacking survives narrow width at 200% zoom (WCAG 1.4.10)' — WCAG 1.4.10 is cited but WCAG 1.4.3 (contrast) is not.
ABSENCE: no AC or scope line asserts that the inline CSS achieves a specific contrast ratio or will be checked with a contrast tool.
findings.finding: The plan commits to WCAG 2.1 AA but provides no observable done-definition for colour contrast (WCAG 1.4.3 — minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text). The visual system section specifies that 'saturated colour [is] reserved for verdict + decision-series chips' but names no target contrast ratio, leaving the colour palette unverifiable against the stated AA standard.
findings.location: What section ('Visual system') and Approach rationale ('single-column stacking survives narrow width at 200% zoom (WCAG 1.4.10)')
scenarios: The alert-hue blocking colour and its white label text are chosen for visual impact but fall below 4.5:1 — a known risk with saturated reds/oranges used as chip backgrounds.
The 'neutral grey' body text on a white page achieves only 3:1, below the AA threshold for normal-sized text.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Visual system bullet: 'All text/background colour pairs meet WCAG 1.4.3 AA (4.5:1 for body text; 3:1 for large text ≥18pt/14pt bold and for non-text UI components). Oracle: axe-core or Pa11y run in the E2E suite on a rendered page asserts zero contrast violations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the three novel architectural decisions (finding-centric IA, neutral threshold meter rationale, convergence viz form-switch threshold) so the decisions survive story closure.
evidence: 'Finding-centric IA (finding = row; four passes = its in-place provenance) was chosen over pass-as-navigation after HCD critique'
'The neutral threshold meter replaces a signed green/red delta whose green=cleared-bar reading collided with the traffic-light convention'
The Scope section lists only server.py and templates/*.html — no ADR, no CLAUDE.md, no design-doc update file.
ABSENCE: no 'docs/adr/' or 'CLAUDE.md' entry in the file list
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel, HCD-critiqued architectural decision — finding-centric IA over pass-as-navigation, the neutral threshold meter design, and the convergence viz form-switching heuristic — but does not commit to capturing these decisions in an ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update. The 'Approach rationale' section lives only in the story ticket, which will not be discoverable once the story is closed.
findings.location: Approach rationale section; Scope section (no ADR/doc file listed)
scenarios: A future contributor changes the convergence viz or threshold meter without understanding the HCD rationale, reintroducing the green=blocked confusion the design explicitly avoided.
The finding-centric IA pattern is re-debated during a follow-on story because the decision record no longer exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an entry to docs/adr/ (or CLAUDE.md under an 'Audit UI decisions' heading) summarizing: (1) finding-centric IA rationale, (2) neutral threshold meter replacing signed delta, (3) ≤3/≥4 round heuristic for bar vs. line chart. List the file in the Scope section so it is part of the deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the convergence viz form-switch threshold (currently 3) into a named constant in server.py (e.g., `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3`) passed to the template, so it is tunable in one place.
evidence: '≤3 rounds → `<div class="conv-bars">`'
'≥4 rounds → `<svg class="conv-line">`'
No config constant, template variable, or server-side setting is named for this threshold.
findings.finding: The ≤3 / ≥4 round threshold governing whether the convergence visualization renders as small-multiple bars or a polyline SVG is hardcoded in the plan with no configuration point. This is a UI heuristic likely to be revisited as real ticket data accumulates (e.g., if 3-round tickets are common but still visually cluttered as bars, or the cutoff is tuned per product feedback).
findings.location: What section — 'Per-round convergence visualization adapts to round count with a STABLE per-form DOM marker: ≤3 rounds → … ≥4 rounds → …'
scenarios: Product feedback after launch requests the cutoff be changed to ≤5; the value must be found and changed in template logic rather than a named constant.
A future story extending the visualization cannot find where the threshold lives without grepping templates.
findings.suggested_fix: In server.py define `CONV_BAR_MAX_ROUNDS = 3` and pass it as template context; the template branches on `rounds|length <= conv_bar_max_rounds`. Document the constant's intent in the ADR/CLAUDE.md entry above.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit error-handling spec for the `/ticket/<id>` route: define behavior for ticket-not-found (404 + human-readable message), read-layer I/O failure (5xx + error page), and malformed payload (logged warning + partial render or graceful error), so auditors always get an actionable response.
evidence: Plan states 'A ticket with no code review and no completion verdict shows an explicit plan-review-only page' — covers absent data only
No mention of HTTP 404 for unknown ticket_id, 500 handling, or user-facing error page when audit_trail() raises
No retry/backoff or graceful-degradation strategy for the read-layer dependency is specified
Acceptance Criteria test fixtures all assume well-formed, present data
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error-handling path for failures in the `audit_trail()` read call (e.g., ticket not found, underlying store unavailable, malformed payload). The 'empty/partial states' section covers only logically absent gate data, not I/O errors or exceptions from the read layer itself.
findings.location: Scope / Compatibility & rollback / 'Empty/partial states' section
scenarios: A user navigates to /ticket/<id> for a non-existent ticket — undefined behavior (silent crash vs. opaque 500)
The underlying data store is transiently unavailable — the route crashes with no user-visible feedback and no logged context
findings.suggested_fix: In the server.py route, wrap the `audit_trail()` call in a try/except: raise HTTP 404 with a 'Ticket not found' page when the ticket is absent, and return a 500 error page (with the exception logged at ERROR level) for unexpected failures. Document these paths in Acceptance Criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal observability requirement to the server.py scope: at minimum, log at ERROR level on any exception from `audit_trail()` or template rendering, and emit a structured log line (ticket_id, gate, round) per request so failures are diagnosable without a debugger.
evidence: No mention of request logging, error-level logging, or metrics in the server.py scope description
No AC line asserts that failures produce a log entry or metric
Reliability section in plan is entirely absent — no logging/alerting design anywhere in the document
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new server route and read-layer integration but specifies no observability instrumentation (no logging, no metrics, no tracing) for the new failure points, making it impossible for operators to detect or debug errors in production-like environments.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/audit/server.py`
scenarios: The read layer silently returns malformed data; the template crashes; no log entry exists to diagnose the failure
Route latency spikes go undetected because no timing instrumentation is present

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an oracle to AC6 (or a new AC) asserting that requesting an out-of-range round index (e.g. `?plan_round=999`) and a non-integer value (e.g. `?plan_round=abc`) both server-render the latest round's content without error.
evidence: Plan body: 'The server clamps an out-of-range or non-integer n to the latest round.'

AC6 oracle: 'seeding ≥2 rounds, asserting the selector shows N, defaults to latest, and requesting an older round renders its (distinct-from-latest) findings' — no assertion for out-of-range or non-integer n.
ABSENCE: no AC mentions ?plan_round=99, ?plan_round=0, ?plan_round=abc, or negative values.
findings.finding: The round-selector invalid-input path is specified in the plan ('The server clamps an out-of-range or non-integer n to the latest round') but no AC or oracle covers this behavior — the round-selector ACs test only the happy path (valid n, defaults to latest).
findings.location: AC: '◂ round n of N ▸ selector' (AC line 6 / Round navigation section)
scenarios: A user or crawler manipulates the URL to ?plan_round=999 or ?plan_round=abc — the server is specified to clamp, but this is untested, so a regression in the clamping logic would go undetected.
A downstream link generator produces an off-by-one round index — the clamped behavior would silently serve stale data without the test catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC6's oracle: 'additionally, requesting ?plan_round=999 and ?plan_round=abc each renders the same content as the default (latest round) and returns HTTP 200.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether Jinja2 is justified over the established f-string HTML generation pattern (see `build_host_html` in `editor.py`); if Jinja2 is chosen, add it to the correct `pyproject.toml` extra and document why the f-string approach is insufficient for the audit page's complexity.
evidence: grep: zero hits for 'import jinja2' or 'from jinja2' anywhere in src/rebar/
pyproject.toml: Jinja2 is absent from all extras (mcp, reviewbot, agents, eval, tracing, grounding, dev)
src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor.py:296 — existing loopback server HTML is generated via `build_host_html()` using Python f-strings (stdlib-only, no template engine dep)
src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py — the sibling handler serving the existing loopback page uses no template engine
Rule-of-Three: 0 existing Jinja2 call-sites; introducing a dependency for a single new module is premature when the established approach already satisfies the need
findings.finding: The plan introduces Jinja2 as a net-new templating dependency (zero existing call-sites in the codebase) when the established pattern for loopback server HTML generation is Python f-string interpolation — as used in `editor.py` / `editor_server.py` (`build_host_html`). Jinja2 is not declared in `pyproject.toml` under any existing extra and has no prior call-site, failing the Rule-of-Three and conflicting with the NIH check (the f-string approach already handles the same use-case for the workflow editor's loopback page).
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'
scenarios: An implementer adds Jinja2 to pyproject.toml (or an existing extra), creating an unplanned transitive dep for everyone who installs that extra.

The scaffolding story's server (the depends_on) may already have chosen a rendering approach (f-strings or a different template engine); Jinja2 would then be a second, divergent approach in the same module.
If the audit server extra is separate from reviewbot, Jinja2 lands in core deps or requires a new extra definition — both broaden the install surface unexpectedly.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the same f-string / stdlib HTML generation approach as `build_host_html` in `editor.py`, factoring the page into named helper functions (one per logical partial). Jinja2 is warranted only if the template logic is genuinely too complex for f-strings — in which case declare it in a new `audit` extra in `pyproject.toml` and note that in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the per-file size cap in the Scope section to match the project's actual 800-LOC soft cap (per `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`), and drop or adjust the '< 100 LOC minimum partial' constraint so splits are driven by real template seams rather than an arbitrary count.
evidence: .github/module-size-ceilings.txt: grandfathered files have ceilings ranging from 806 to 1083 LOC, establishing the actual soft cap as 800 LOC
.github/module-size-allowlist.txt header: references '800-LOC soft cap (docs/architecture.md)'
Plan states '< 500 LOC' with no citation to the project policy document
The plan's own '< 100 LOC' minimum partial size implicitly creates 5–8 partial files to stay under 500 LOC, when 1–2 files under 800 LOC would satisfy the real policy
findings.finding: The plan states a `< 500 LOC` per-file cap for templates ('each file within module-size policy (< 500 LOC; split partials along real seams, none < 100 LOC)'), but the project's actual soft cap is **800 LOC** (enforced by `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`). The plan-invented 500-LOC constraint is stricter than the real policy and could drive premature splitting of template partials into more files than necessary, violating YAGNI by creating fragmentation overhead to satisfy a non-existent rule.
findings.location: Scope section: 'each file within module-size policy (< 500 LOC; split partials along real seams, none < 100 LOC)'
scenarios: Implementer splits template HTML across 6–8 partials to stay under 500 LOC, creating fragmented files with low cohesion that the real 800-LOC policy would not require.
A reviewer flags a 600-LOC template file as policy-violating based on the plan's stated cap, when the project gate would pass it.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '< 500 LOC; split partials along real seams, none < 100 LOC' with 'each file within the project's 800-LOC soft cap; split partials along real seams (e.g. gate-section, finding-row, completion-panel, convergence-viz each as a distinct partial where a natural boundary exists)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all expected template partial file paths in `file_impact` (gate-section, finding-provenance, completion-panel, and empty-state partials in addition to `ticket.html` and `convergence.svg.html`) so the module-size policy can be verified per file.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2) — the page + sticky gate-strip + gate-section + finding-provenance + completion-panel + empty-state partials; ... Each file within module-size policy (< 500 LOC; split partials along real seams, none < 100 LOC).'

Ticket file_impact lists only: {path: 'src/rebar/audit/templates/ticket.html'} and {path: 'src/rebar/audit/templates/convergence.svg.html'} — no gate-section, finding-provenance, completion-panel, or empty-state partials.
ABSENCE: gate-section partial, finding-provenance partial, completion-panel partial, and empty-state partial are named in the plan body but absent from file_impact.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section calls for multiple Jinja2 template partials ('page + sticky gate-strip + gate-section + finding-provenance + completion-panel + empty-state partials', each < 500 LOC, none < 100 LOC), but the ticket's `file_impact` metadata enumerates only two template paths (`src/rebar/audit/templates/ticket.html` and `src/rebar/audit/templates/convergence.svg.html`), omitting the gate-section, finding-provenance, completion-panel, and empty-state partial files the plan body explicitly requires. An implementer consulting `file_impact` to enumerate files to create or review against the module-size policy will miss several expected files.
findings.location: Scope section / ticket `file_impact` metadata
scenarios: An implementer counts files from file_impact and creates only ticket.html + convergence.svg.html, stuffing all partial content into ticket.html — violating the < 500 LOC module-size policy.
A reviewer auditing file_impact to confirm module-size compliance will not find the partial files to check.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `file_impact`: `src/rebar/audit/templates/gate_section.html`, `src/rebar/audit/templates/finding_row.html`, `src/rebar/audit/templates/completion_panel.html`, and `src/rebar/audit/templates/empty_state.html` (exact names TBD by implementer, but they must be listed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the coaching join algorithm in the finding-provenance section: the server must scan the payload-level `coaching[]` list and collect notes where `finding.id ∈ note.finding_refs`, then render the matched note(s) inline in the finding row — for both plan-review and code-review findings.
evidence: Plan: 'A finding row (chevron = expandable) opens IN PLACE to the provenance read as ONE narrative … Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)'

sidecar.py _slim(f) returns no coaching field: the persisted finding dict has id/norm_id/location/criteria/decision/priority/reason/verification/impact/evidence/scenarios/block_threshold/blocking_enabled/finding/suggested_fix but NOT coaching
sidecar.py build_payload() stores coaching as a top-level `coaching` key on the payload, with each note carrying `finding_refs: list[str]` that references finding `id` values
coach.py coach_listing() uses `f['id']` as the join key — `finding_refs` holds these ids
code_review/sidecar.py build_payload() likewise stores coaching top-level: `'coaching': verdict.get('coaching', [])`
findings.finding: The per-finding coaching join mechanism is unspecified. The plan states each finding row shows its 'pass-4 matched coaching move + note' as part of the four-pass atomic provenance, but the sidecar stores coaching as a TOP-LEVEL array on the payload (`coaching[]{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs[], coaching}`) — not as a field on each finding record. The `_slim(f)` function (the source of every field the page reads) confirms there is no per-finding `coaching` key. The join is: for a given finding, scan the top-level `coaching[]` list and collect notes where `finding.id ∈ note.finding_refs`. The plan never describes this join. An implementer following the plan as written could attempt to read a non-existent `finding.coaching` field, silently rendering no coaching for any finding.
findings.location: What > Plan-review and code-review sections > 'The finding is the atomic unit' paragraph; also 'For code-review findings … the round's matched coaching'
scenarios: Implementer renders finding provenance by reading fields off each finding dict, finds no `coaching` key, silently omits coaching for all findings
Implementer renders coaching as a separate top-level list rather than inline per-finding, breaking the 'finding is the atomic unit' IA goal
Code-review finding coaching is similarly silently dropped because the same join applies but is not described for the code-review case
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'The finding is the atomic unit' paragraph, add: 'Coaching match: the server joins the finding to its coaching note(s) by scanning the payload-level `coaching[]` array for entries where `finding.id` appears in `note.finding_refs`; a finding with no matched note omits the Coaching pass silently (not all findings receive coaching).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why Jinja2 is preferred over the codebase's established `HTMLResponse` + f-string pattern (editor.py precedent) for this single read-only page, OR acknowledge the choice and name the `pyproject.toml` extra (e.g., a new `[audit]` extra or extension of `[reviewbot]`) that will declare `jinja2` as a direct dependency.
evidence: grep jinja2 across src/ → zero hits (confirmed by search)
pyproject.toml has no `jinja2` entry in `[project.dependencies]` or any `[project.optional-dependencies]` extra
`reviewbot` extra = `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — no Jinja2
`src/rebar/llm/workflow/editor_server.py` and `editor.py` use stdlib `http.server` + Python f-strings for HTML rendering — zero Jinja2
`src/rebar/review_bot/app.py` and `opcert_service/app.py` use `JSONResponse` directly — no template engine
`fastapi.responses.HTMLResponse` exists and resolves in the environment, making Jinja2 unnecessary for a single static-ish page
Rule-of-Three: 0 existing Jinja2 call-sites → novelty criterion applies
findings.finding: The plan mandates Jinja2 file-based templates (`src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html`) as the rendering engine, but Jinja2 has zero prior call-sites in `src/rebar` and is not declared in any `pyproject.toml` extra. The codebase's established prior art for serving HTML from Python is (a) stdlib f-strings + `http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler` (`editor.py` / `editor_server.py`), and (b) bare `fastapi.responses.HTMLResponse` / `JSONResponse` (reviewbot, opcert_service — both avoid a template engine entirely). The plan introduces Jinja2 without acknowledging these prior-art alternatives or justifying why a full template engine is preferred over the established patterns for a single read-only page.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New `src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html` (Jinja2)'; Approach rationale: 'server-rendered from the read layer over native no-JS `<details>`'
scenarios: An executor adds `jinja2` to the `reviewbot` extra (polluting an unrelated surface) or assumes it is transitively available through FastAPI's optional dep on aiofiles, making the audit page silently fragile when that transitive changes.

A future refactor of the `reviewbot` extra drops aiofiles/jinja2 transitively and silently breaks the audit server with an opaque `ModuleNotFoundError` instead of the project's standard `OptionalDependencyError`.

An implementer using the established `HTMLResponse` + Jinja2-free f-string pattern from editor.py discovers the plan mandates Jinja2 and must choose between following the plan or the codebase's prior art.
findings.suggested_fix: In 'Approach rationale', add one sentence: 'Jinja2 is chosen over the editor.py f-string pattern because the audit page's conditional/loop-heavy rendering (finding groups, round selectors, convergence viz) makes a logic-less f-string fragile to maintain; `jinja2` will be declared in a new `[audit]` extra in `pyproject.toml`.' Then add `jinja2>=3` to a new `[project.optional-dependencies]` `audit` extra in the Scope file list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `<details>/<summary>` AT support (collapsed/expanded state announcement) against the team's target screen reader / browser matrix, or add a noted caveat in the plan acknowledging the known NVDA+Firefox gap and whether it is acceptable given the auditor/debugger audience.
evidence: Plan quote: 'keyboard + screen-reader support come free'
Plan cites WCAG 1.1.1 (non-text content) and WCAG 1.4.10 (reflow) but does not acknowledge AT <details> support gaps
ABSENCE: no AT compatibility matrix, browser support note, or aria-expanded fallback is mentioned in the plan
Known AT inconsistency: <details> collapsed/expanded state is not reliably announced by NVDA+Firefox or older JAWS — documented in WebAIM AT browser matrix and MDN browser compat table
Zero prior call-sites of <details>/<summary> in src/rebar to anchor the claim against tested environments
findings.finding: The plan asserts that native `<details>/<summary>` gives 'keyboard + screen-reader support come free' — an unverified capability claim. AT support for `<details>/<summary>` (specifically the expanded/collapsed state announcement) is documented as inconsistent across NVDA+Firefox and older JAWS versions. The plan simultaneously commits to WCAG 1.1.1 and WCAG 1.4.10, but relies on this unverified claim for ALL finding-row collapsibles — the page's primary interaction surface — without citing any AT compatibility matrix or browser support caveat.
findings.location: What section: 'built from native `<details>/<summary>` (no JS — keyboard + screen-reader support come free)'; Approach rationale: 'native no-JS `<details>` + jump-linked anchors'
scenarios: An auditor using NVDA+Firefox expands a finding row but receives no state-change announcement, silently breaking the provenance-read flow without the page author realizing it.

WCAG compliance claim fails a future accessibility audit, requiring a post-ship `aria-expanded` + role augmentation retrofit that conflicts with the no-JS constraint (JS is needed to toggle aria-expanded dynamically when JS is absent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale in 'Approach rationale' confirming that server-side SVG/chart libraries compatible with the no-external-JS constraint were considered and rejected (e.g., 'Matplotlib/pygal add a heavy dep for a two-series polyline; inline SVG with hardcoded viewBox math is sufficient') so the custom chart choice satisfies the anti-repackaging criterion.
evidence: Plan quote: 'the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)' — the no-external-JS constraint is named but alternatives within that constraint are not
No mention of server-side SVG libraries (Matplotlib, pygal, svgwrite) or CSS-only bar chart patterns in the Approach rationale
ABSENCE: 'Approach rationale' justifies the ≤3/≥4 form-switch by outcome ('adapts to round count') but not by prior-art survey
findings.finding: The plan introduces a dual-mode convergence visualization (≤3 rounds → `<div class="conv-bars">` small multiples; ≥4 rounds → `<svg class="conv-line">` polyline chart) hand-rolled as inline SVG in a Jinja2 template, without acknowledging whether server-side SVG/chart libraries compatible with the no-external-JS constraint (e.g., Matplotlib SVG export, pygal, or pure-CSS bar charts) were evaluated and rejected. The novelty of hand-rolling a branching chart form has no prior-art acknowledgment.
findings.location: Scope section: 'the convergence visualization + threshold meter as inline SVG/CSS (no external JS/asset)'; What section: '≤3 rounds → `<div class="conv-bars">` … ≥4 rounds → `<svg class="conv-line">`'
scenarios: A future maintainer adding rounds encounters label-overlap or viewBox scaling bugs in the hand-rolled polyline that a library would have handled, with no record of whether libraries were evaluated.
The dual-mode branching adds test surface (two E2E paths per gate) that a single CSS-bar approach would have collapsed to one.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to 'Approach rationale': 'Server-side SVG libraries (Matplotlib SVG export, pygal, svgwrite) were considered for the convergence viz but rejected — they add multi-MB transitive deps for a chart that is two `<polyline>` elements at most; the inline SVG approach is intentionally minimal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the web framework the audit server will use (FastAPI reusing [reviewbot], or standalone jinja2 with a new [audit] extra, or stdlib http.server) and add the necessary pyproject.toml extra/dep so the audit server's runtime requirements are explicit and installable without the unrelated [reviewbot] extra.
evidence: Plan: 'src/rebar/audit/server.py — add a /ticket/<id> route consuming rebar.audit.read.audit_trail'
Plan: 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'
pyproject.toml: jinja2 is NOT listed in [project.dependencies] or any optional extra; fastapi appears only under [reviewbot]
fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates: EXISTS (verified installed)
jinja2.Environment: EXISTS (verified installed, as a transitive of fastapi/starlette)
src/rebar/audit/ contains only __init__.py and read.py — no server.py exists
Evidence class: Partially-verified — Jinja2 capability exists but the specific framework surface (which extra, which app factory) is unconfirmed
findings.finding: The plan names Jinja2 as the template engine and targets src/rebar/audit/server.py, but does not declare which web framework wraps it (FastAPI, stdlib http.server, or other), and no pyproject.toml extra covers the audit server's runtime dependencies. If FastAPI is used (as in review_bot), it lives only in the [reviewbot] optional extra — an unrelated extra an audit-only deployer may not install. If jinja2 standalone is used without FastAPI, jinja2 itself is not declared in [project.dependencies] or any [audit] extra, making it an undeclared runtime requirement. Either path leaves the audit server's dependency chain unresolved in the package metadata.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/audit/server.py' + 'New src/rebar/audit/templates/*.html (Jinja2)'; pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies]
scenarios: A deployer installs nava-rebar without the [reviewbot] extra (a common case for an audit-only setup) and imports rebar.audit.server — the fastapi/jinja2 import fails with ImportError because neither is in core deps.

The depends_on server-scaffolding story chooses stdlib http.server + bare jinja2, but jinja2 is not listed as a dep — it works only because fastapi happens to be installed, creating an invisible transitive dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new `[project.optional-dependencies]` entry, e.g. `audit = ["jinja2>=3"]` (if using standalone jinja2 + stdlib http), or extend `[reviewbot]` to cover the audit server if FastAPI is reused, and name the chosen framework explicitly in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how the Coaching slot in each finding row is populated: state that a coaching note is matched to a finding when the finding's `id` appears in the coaching note's `finding_refs[]` list (scanning the top-level `coaching[]` array on the round payload — the field is not embedded on the finding dict itself).
evidence: Plan: 'A finding row (chevron = expandable) opens IN PLACE to the provenance read as ONE narrative … Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)' — no join algorithm stated

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py _slim(f) function (lines 365–419): the persisted per-finding dict has id/norm_id/location/criteria/decision/priority/reason/verification/impact/evidence/scenarios/block_threshold/blocking_enabled/finding/suggested_fix — NO coaching key
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py build_payload() (lines 445–454): coaching stored at payload top-level as coaching[], each note carrying finding_refs: list[str]
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py coach_listing() (line 120): uses f['id'] as the listing key — confirming finding_refs holds these id values
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py build_payload() (line 111): code-review coaching also stored top-level with finding_refs
findings.finding: The plan specifies that each finding row expands to show 'Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)' as part of its provenance, but never defines the join algorithm by which a coaching note is matched to a finding. In the sidecar schema, coaching[] is a TOP-LEVEL payload array (not embedded per-finding); each note carries finding_refs: list[str] referencing the finding's id field. The join rule is: collect coaching notes where the finding's id appears in note.finding_refs. Without this rule stated, a template implementer reading the plan cannot know whether to read a per-finding 'coaching' key (which does not exist on the finding dict — confirmed by sidecar.py _slim(f)), scan the top-level list by norm_id, or something else.
findings.location: What section — 'The finding is the atomic unit' paragraph: 'Coaching (pass-4 matched `coaching` move + note)'; also code-review finding row: 'the round's matched `coaching`'
scenarios: A template implementer reads the plan's 'Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)' spec and looks for a coaching field on the finding dict — finds none, silently omits coaching for all findings.
An implementer joins by norm_id instead of id, producing incorrect or empty coaching associations since finding_refs carries id values, not norm_ids.
A code-review finding row implementer applies a different join key (e.g. finding.finding text) because the plan provides no algorithm.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'The finding is the atomic unit' paragraph: 'The Coaching slot is populated by scanning the round payload's top-level `coaching[]` array and collecting notes where the finding's `id` value appears in `note.finding_refs` — coaching is not a field on the finding record itself.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `verdict` (the top-level pass/fail string, present on both code_review_result_v1 and code_review_result_v2 payloads) to the Dependencies section's field inventory for `trail['code_reviews']` sidecars, so the CODE chip's source is unambiguous.
evidence: Dependencies: 'each sidecar is a code-review payload (the reader accepts BOTH code_review_result_v1 and code_review_result_v2) with SEPARATE buckets blocking/advisory (+ dropped/indeterminate/coaching on v2)' — verdict is absent from this field list
What/Gate strip: 'CODE from the latest sidecar of the code-review artifact in code_reviews (its `verdict` + blocking count)' — verdict is named as the source
src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py build_payload() line 97: 'verdict': verdict.get('verdict') — field exists in both v1 and v2 payloads
tests/unit/test_code_review_artifact.py line 316: v1 payload fixture carries 'verdict': 'PASS' — confirms field present in both schema versions
findings.finding: The Dependencies section's field inventory for `trail['code_reviews']` sidecars omits the `verdict` field, yet the What section's gate-strip specification relies on it ('CODE from the latest sidecar of the code-review artifact in `code_reviews` (its `verdict` + blocking count)'). An implementer reading only the Dependencies contract cannot know this field exists on code-review sidecar payloads, creating an unresolved source for the CODE chip's pass/fail state.
findings.location: Dependencies section — `trail['code_reviews']` field description; What section — 'Gate-status strip (sticky)' CODE chip derivation rule
scenarios: An implementer reads the Dependencies contract, finds no `verdict` field listed for code-review sidecars, and synthesises a verdict from bucket-emptiness (e.g. blocking==[] ⟹ PASS) rather than reading the stored field — producing a different result if the stored verdict disagrees with bucket counts.
An implementer omits the CODE chip's pass/fail label entirely, showing only a count.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dependencies section, extend the code-review sidecar description to: 'each sidecar is a code-review payload (accepts BOTH code_review_result_v1 and code_review_result_v2) carrying a top-level `verdict` field plus SEPARATE buckets blocking/advisory (+ dropped/indeterminate/coaching on v2).'

findings.finding: The convergence-viz AC's oracle for the visually-hidden screen-reader table is ambiguous: 'the visually-hidden `<table>` rows equal the per-round counts' is not a parseable assertion. It is unclear whether (a) the number of `<tr>` rows equals the number of rounds, (b) the number of rows equals the number of series, or (c) individual cell values equal the per-round-per-series counts. As written, an executor cannot write a deterministic test assertion from this phrase.
findings.location: AC #6 — 'the visually-hidden `<table>` rows equal the per-round counts'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the visually-hidden `<table>` rows equal the per-round counts' with: 'the visually-hidden `<table>` has exactly N data rows (one per round) and 4 data columns (one per canonical series); for the 2-round fixture the table cell at (round 1, BLOCKING) contains the expected blocking count from that round's fixture data.'

findings.finding: The plan states that plan-review findings carry `decision` values from the set `block|advisory|overflow|indeterminate|dropped`, but the codebase's `pass3_decide` (the sole source of the `decision` field) only emits `block`, `advisory`, `dropped`, `indeterminate`. Overflow findings land in the sidecar's `overflow` bucket but retain `decision == 'advisory'` — no code anywhere stamps `decision = 'overflow'` on a finding. An implementer writing grouping logic keyed on `decision == 'overflow'` will silently find zero such findings; the 'merging overflow into advisory' instruction is vacuously satisfied (all overflow findings already have `decision == 'advisory'`) but the description is misleading.
findings.location: Dependencies section: 'Each finding carries … `decision` (`block|advisory|overflow|indeterminate|dropped`…)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `decision` (`block|advisory|overflow|indeterminate|dropped`) with `decision` (`block|advisory|dropped|indeterminate`) and add a note: 'Overflow findings are those in the sidecar `overflow` bucket; they carry `decision == "advisory"` — the ADVISORY canonical series automatically includes them.'

findings.finding: The round-selector query-parameter indexing scheme is never stated. The plan uses `?plan_round=2` as an example and says the list is 'newest-first', but does not specify whether n is 1-indexed (n=1 = newest) or 0-indexed (n=0 = newest). An executor implementing the server route and the AC fixture will independently choose a convention, and a mismatch between the route, the selector template, and the AC fixture will cause a silent off-by-one rather than a failing test.
findings.location: Scope section — 'a tiny per-gate round selector reading `?<gate>_round=<n>`'; AC #7 — 'requesting an older round (via `?<gate>_round=<n>`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope bullet: 'The `n` parameter is 1-indexed against the newest-first list (n=1 = latest round; n=N = oldest); an out-of-range or absent value falls back to n=1.' Update AC #7 to say: 'for a 2-round fixture, `?plan_round=2` (the oldest round) renders findings distinct from `?plan_round=1` (the default latest).'

findings.finding: The gate-status strip must show a CODE gate verdict (PASS / FAIL / 'not run'), but `trail['code_reviews']` from the read layer is a LIST of code-review artifact entries (each with their own `sidecars[]`), not a single aggregated verdict. The plan never specifies the aggregation rule: how the server route should derive a single CODE gate verdict + blocking count from multiple code-review sidecar records. For example: does the strip show FAIL if any sidecar has blocking findings? Does it show the latest sidecar's verdict? What if sidecars across multiple code-review artifacts conflict?
findings.location: What section — 'Gate-status strip (sticky)'; Dependencies — `trail['code_reviews']` shape

findings.finding: The plan describes that a finding row's Coaching pass renders 'the round's matched coaching move + note' for both plan-review and code-review, but never specifies the join algorithm — i.e., how the template identifies which `coaching[]` entries are 'matched' to a given finding row. The sidecar stores coaching as a top-level list with `finding_refs: list[str]` (finding `id`s); matching requires scanning `coaching[].finding_refs` for the finding's `id`. This join protocol is load-bearing for the template but absent from the plan.
findings.location: What section: 'Coaching (pass-4 matched `coaching` move + note)' and 'the round's matched `coaching`' for code-review rows
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'finding is the atomic unit' paragraph, add: 'Coaching (pass-4): filter `coaching[]` for entries where `finding_refs` contains this finding\'s `id`; render each matched entry as move name + coaching prose. Omit the pass entirely when no match exists.'

findings.finding: The plan names a 'four-series mapping helper' in `server.py` that maps findings from both plan-review and code-review payloads to the four canonical series, but never defines its contract for code-review payloads. Code-review findings have no per-finding `decision` field — series membership is determined solely by which bucket key (`blocking`/`advisory`/`dropped`/`indeterminate`) contains them in the sidecar. The helper must handle two different shapes (plan-review: flat `findings[]` array with per-finding `decision` field vs. code-review: four separate bucket lists), but this dual-shape protocol is unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section: 'server.py — add … a tiny per-gate round selector … and the four-series mapping helper'; What section: 'Code-review findings are NOT the plan-review four-pass shape — they carry the fields THEY have'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section's `server.py` bullet: 'The four-series helper accepts either shape: (a) plan-review — iterates `findings[]`, reads `f["decision"]` to assign series; (b) code-review — iterates each of `blocking/advisory/dropped/indeterminate` bucket lists, assigning series by bucket key. Returns `{series: [findings]}` in both cases.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that finding-row provenance includes a 'Coaching (pass-4 matched coaching move + note)' slot for both plan-review and code-review sections, but never defines the match protocol — specifically that coaching notes are joined to findings via `finding_refs` (the coaching note's `finding_refs[]` list, matched against the finding's `id`). Without this join-key rule, a template implementer has no algorithm for populating the Coaching slot of a code-review (or plan-review) finding row.
findings.location: What section — 'The finding is the atomic unit' / finding-row provenance description; Approach rationale — 'passes are a pipeline artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'The finding is the atomic unit' description: 'Coaching match: a coaching move is associated with a finding when the finding's `id` is present in the coaching note's `finding_refs[]`; if no coaching note matches, the Coaching slot is omitted from the provenance block.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
